title: Atomic Structure, Number, Weight, and Isotopes
narrationScript: Picture the atom as a tiny solar system. At the center sits the nucleus, a dense knot of protons and neutrons that holds nearly all of the atom's mass in almost none of its volume. If an atom were the size of a football stadium, the nucleus would be a marble at the fifty yard line. Around that nucleus, electrons travel in defined shells, and each shell is a distinct energy level with a strict occupancy limit. We letter the shells from the inside out. K, then L, then M, then N and beyond. Each shell holds at most two n squared electrons, where n is the shell number counted from the center. So the K shell holds two, the L shell holds eight, the M shell holds eighteen, and the N shell holds thirty two. Electrons fill from the inside out, and the outermost electrons, called valence electrons, do the chemistry. Inner shell electrons are bound tightly to the nucleus, and the energy needed to knock one loose, the binding energy, will matter a great deal when we study how X rays are produced and absorbed. Pull an electron out of an atom and you leave behind a charged ion. That act, ionization, is the signature of the radiation we work with. Two numbers describe every atom you will ever meet. The first is the atomic number, written as Z. It is simply the count of protons in the nucleus, and it tells you which element you have. One proton is hydrogen. Twenty seven protons is cobalt. Seventy seven is iridium. Eighty two is lead. Change the proton count and you have changed the element itself. The second number is the mass number, written as A. It is the count of protons plus neutrons together, the total head count of the nucleus. Subtract atomic number from mass number and you get the neutron count. Cobalt sixty has a mass number of sixty and an atomic number of twenty seven, so it carries thirty three neutrons. You will also hear about atomic weight, which is related but not identical. Atomic weight is the average mass of an element's atoms as they occur in nature, weighted by how common each variety is. That is why chlorine's atomic weight is about thirty five and a half rather than a whole number. Nature stocks two versions of chlorine, and the average lands between them. Those versions bring us to the key idea of this lesson. Atoms of the same element must have the same number of protons, but they may carry different numbers of neutrons. These variations are isotopes. Same atomic number, different mass number. Same chemistry, different nucleus. Hydrogen comes three ways. Plain hydrogen with no neutron, deuterium with one, and tritium with two. Every chemical reaction treats all three alike, but their nuclei behave very differently. And that difference is everything to us. Some combinations of protons and neutrons are stable and last forever. Other combinations are unbalanced. The nucleus holds excess energy, and sooner or later it rearranges itself and throws that energy out as radiation. An unstable isotope is called a radioisotope, and the throwing out process is radioactive decay. Cobalt fifty nine, the only natural form of cobalt, is perfectly stable. Put it in a nuclear reactor, let each nucleus capture one extra neutron, and it becomes cobalt sixty, a radioisotope with a half life of about five and a quarter years that emits the energetic gamma rays we use to shoot thick steel. Iridium one ninety one becomes iridium one ninety two the same way, with a half life of about seventy four days. That is the workhorse source of pipeline and plant radiography. Selenium seventy five, with a half life of about one hundred twenty days and gentler energy, covers thin wall, high contrast work. So keep the distinction sharp. Isotope is a neutral word. Every element has isotopes, and most of the atoms around you are stable ones. A radioisotope is an unstable isotope, and that instability is exactly what we harvest. A gamma source is nothing more than a carefully packaged collection of unstable nuclei decaying on their own fixed schedule, whether or not anyone is watching. In the next module we follow that decay and the radiation it throws off.
imagePrompt: A technician in a lead-lined source storage room uses long-handled tongs to transfer a small stainless steel isotope capsule from a lead container, isotope decay charts pinned on the wall behind him.

sections.heading: The Nuclear Atom
sections.body:
The working model of the atom is a miniature solar system. At the center sits the **nucleus** - protons and neutrons packed into a region about 10,000 times smaller than the atom itself, yet holding nearly all its mass. If the atom were a stadium, the nucleus would be a marble on the fifty-yard line; the rest is the electrons' territory.

Electrons occupy defined **shells**, lettered **K, L, M, N...** from the nucleus outward. Each shell is an energy level with a strict capacity of **2n²** electrons, where n is the shell number: K holds 2, L holds 8, M holds 18, N holds 32. Shells fill from the inside out, and the outermost (**valence**) electrons govern chemical behavior.

Every electron is held by a **binding energy** - strongest for inner shells of heavy elements. Supply more than that energy and the electron is ejected, leaving a positive **ion**. This matters twice in radiography: knocking inner-shell electrons around is how X-ray tubes generate characteristic radiation, and ionization in the test part, the film, the detector, and your own tissue is how radiation acts on matter.

sections.caption: Bohr-model shells with their 2n² capacities, and standard nuclide notation decoded for cobalt-60.

sections.heading: Atomic Number: The Element's Identity
sections.body:
The **atomic number, Z**, is the count of protons in the nucleus - nothing more, and nothing less than the element's identity. One proton is hydrogen. Twenty-seven is cobalt. Seventy-seven is iridium. Eighty-two is lead. Ninety-two is uranium. The periodic table is simply the elements lined up in order of Z.

Change the proton count and you change the element itself - something no chemical reaction can do, but exactly what nuclear processes do. In a neutral atom, Z also equals the number of orbital electrons, so atomic number ties the nuclear core to the chemistry. For the radiographer, Z is a preview of absorption: higher-Z materials interact more strongly with radiation, which is why lead (Z = 82) shields and tungsten (Z = 74) inclusions print bright white on a weld radiograph while the surrounding iron (Z = 26) passes far more of the beam.

sections.heading: Mass Number and Atomic Weight
sections.body:
The **mass number, A**, is the total count of nucleons - protons plus neutrons. Neutron count is found by subtraction: **N = A − Z**. Standard nuclide notation writes A as a superscript and Z as a subscript ahead of the symbol; in plain text we write Co-60 or Ir-192.

- Cobalt-60: A = 60, Z = 27, so N = 60 − 27 = **33 neutrons**.
- Iridium-192: A = 192, Z = 77, so N = 192 − 77 = **115 neutrons**.

**Atomic weight** is a different, related quantity: the average mass of an element's atoms as found in nature, weighted by the abundance of each isotope, expressed in atomic mass units. Mass number is always a whole number for a specific nuclide; atomic weight usually is not, because it is an average across isotopes. Chlorine is the classic example: nature supplies roughly three parts chlorine-35 to one part chlorine-37, so the atomic weight lands at about **35.45**. When an exam question asks how many neutrons a nuclide has, use the mass number, never the atomic weight.

sections.caption: Reading nuclides: Z, N, and A for atoms a radiographer meets.

sections.heading: Isotopes: Same Element, Different Nucleus
sections.body:
Atoms of one element must all carry the same number of protons - but they may carry **different numbers of neutrons**. Such variants are **isotopes**: same Z, different A. Hydrogen comes three ways: ordinary hydrogen-1 with no neutron, deuterium (H-2) with one, and tritium (H-3) with two. Chemically the three are interchangeable - bonding is electron business, and all have one electron - but their nuclei differ in mass and in stability.

Most elements occur naturally as a blend of isotopes, which is exactly why atomic weights are rarely whole numbers. Natural iridium, for instance, is a mix of stable iridium-191 and iridium-193. Remember the rule: **changing neutrons changes the isotope; changing protons changes the element.**

sections.heading: Radioisotopes: When the Nucleus Cannot Settle
sections.body:
Whether a nucleus is stable depends on the balance between its protons and neutrons. Some combinations sit in equilibrium and last forever. Others hold **excess nuclear energy**: sooner or later the nucleus rearranges and ejects that energy as radiation. An unstable isotope is a **radioisotope**, and the process is **radioactive decay** - spontaneous, statistical, and utterly indifferent to temperature, pressure, or chemistry. Each radioisotope decays at its own fixed rate, described by its **half-life**.

Industrial gamma sources are manufactured by deliberately unbalancing stable nuclei with **neutron activation** in a reactor:

- **Cobalt-59 + neutron → cobalt-60** - half-life about 5.27 years; energetic gammas for thick steel.
- **Iridium-191 + neutron → iridium-192** - half-life about 74 days; the workhorse for common steel thicknesses.
- **Selenium-75** - half-life about 120 days; lower energy for thin-wall, high-contrast work.

A gamma source is simply a packaged collection of unstable nuclei decaying on schedule - which is why it can never be switched off, only shielded and allowed to decay.

sections.caption: One captured neutron turns stable cobalt-59 into cobalt-60, a radioisotope that decays with a 5.27-year half-life and emits the gamma rays used for thick-section radiography.

sections.title: Isotope does not mean radioactive
sections.body: Every element has isotopes, and most isotopes around you are **stable**. Only the unstable ones - **radioisotopes** - decay and emit radiation. Cobalt-59 in tool steel is harmless; cobalt-60 in a source capsule will read on your survey meter from across the yard. Same element, same chemistry, one extra neutron.

checkQuestions.question: Using the 2n² rule, the maximum electron capacities of the K, L, and M shells are:
checkQuestions.explanation: Capacity = 2n²: K (n=1) holds 2, L (n=2) holds 8, M (n=3) holds 18, N (n=4) holds 32.

checkQuestions.question: What single quantity determines which element an atom is?
options: The number of protons (atomic number Z)
checkQuestions.explanation: The proton count is the element's identity. Neutron count can vary (isotopes) and mass changes with it, but 27 protons is cobalt every time.

checkQuestions.question: Iridium-192 has atomic number 77. How many neutrons does its nucleus contain?
checkQuestions.explanation: N = A − Z = 192 − 77 = 115 neutrons. Mass number counts protons plus neutrons, so subtract the protons.

checkQuestions.question: Why is chlorine's atomic weight about 35.45 rather than a whole number?
options: It is a weighted average over the natural mix of chlorine-35 and chlorine-37
Atomic weight includes the mass of the surrounding air
Measurement error in early chemistry that was never corrected
checkQuestions.explanation: Atomic weight averages the masses of an element's naturally occurring isotopes by abundance - roughly three Cl-35 atoms for every Cl-37 - so it lands between the two whole mass numbers.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes an isotope from a radioisotope?
options: All isotopes are radioactive; radioisotope is just the formal term
Isotopes differ in proton count; radioisotopes differ in neutron count
Isotopes are forms of an element differing in neutron count; a radioisotope is an isotope whose unstable nucleus decays and emits radiation
An isotope becomes a radioisotope when it is heated above its critical temperature
checkQuestions.explanation: Isotope is a neutral term - same protons, different neutrons, stable or not. Only the unstable ones decay and emit radiation, and no chemical or thermal process can start or stop that decay.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 2.4 (atomic structure)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 2.5 (atomic number and weight)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 2.6 (isotope versus radioisotope)
Source half-lives: Ir-192 ≈ 74 days, Co-60 ≈ 5.27 years, Se-75 ≈ 120 days (reference values)
